The **Westminster system**, or **Westminster model**, is a type of [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") government modelled on that of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). Key aspects of the system include an [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") made up of members of the legislature which is [responsible](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") to the legislature, the presence of [parliamentary opposition](/wiki/Parliamentary_opposition "Parliamentary opposition") parties, and a ceremonial [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") who is separate from the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). The term derives from the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), the seat of the British parliament. The Westminster system can be contrasted with the [presidential system](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system"), which originated in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) and with the [semi-presidential](/wiki/Semi-presidential "Semi-presidential") system based on the government of [France](/wiki/France "France").

The Westminster system is used, or was once used, in the national and [subnational](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") legislatures of most [former colonies](/wiki/List_of_former_European_colonies "List of former European colonies") of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") upon gaining [self-government](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony"), beginning with the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada") in 1848.[[2]](#cite_note-seidle03-2) However, many former colonies have since adopted other forms of government.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

The Westminster system of government may include some of the following features:[[3]](#cite_note-3)

* A [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") or [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") who functions as the nominal or legal and constitutional holder of executive power, and holds numerous [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power"), but whose daily duties mainly consist of performing ceremonial functions. Examples include King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), the [governors-general](/wiki/Governor-general "Governor-general") in the [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms"), or the presidents of many countries, and [state or provincial](/wiki/State_(country_subdivision) "State (country subdivision)") [governors](/wiki/Governor "Governor") in [federal systems](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism"). Exceptions to this are [Ireland](/wiki/President_of_Ireland "President of Ireland") and [Israel](/wiki/President_of_Israel "President of Israel"), whose presidents are both de jure and de facto ceremonial, and the [latter](/wiki/President_of_Israel "President of Israel") possesses no reserve powers whatsoever.
* A [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") (or head of the executive), known as the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") (PM), [premier](/wiki/Premier "Premier"), [chief minister](/wiki/Chief_minister "Chief minister") or [first minister](/wiki/First_minister "First minister"). While the head of state appoints the head of government, constitutional convention suggests that a majority of elected members of parliament must support the person appointed.[[4]](#cite_note-oba-4) If more than half of elected parliamentarians belong to the same political party, then the parliamentary [leader](/wiki/Leader "Leader") of that party typically is appointed.[[4]](#cite_note-oba-4)
* An [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") led by the head of government usually made up of members of the legislature with the senior members of the executive in a [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)") adhering to the principle of [cabinet collective responsibility](/wiki/Cabinet_collective_responsibility "Cabinet collective responsibility"); such members wield authority on behalf of the nominal or theoretical executive.
* An independent, non-partisan [civil service](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service") that advises on, and implements, decisions of the elected government. Civil servants hold permanent appointments and can expect merit-based selection processes and continuity of employment when governments change.[[5]](#cite_note-apsc-5)
* A [parliamentary opposition](/wiki/Opposition_(parliamentary) "Opposition (parliamentary)") (in a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system")) with an official [leader of the opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_opposition "Leader of the opposition"), which generally takes on an adversarial role,[[6]](#cite_note-6) presenting arguments against the government's policies. In certain countries, the [leader of the opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_opposition "Leader of the opposition") is expected to be ready to form a government if the office of head of government becomes vacant.
* A legislature, often [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism"), with at least one elected house — although [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") systems also exist. Traditionally, the lower house is elected using [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") from single-member districts, which is still more common, although some use a system of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") (e.g. [Israel](/wiki/Politics_of_Israel "Politics of Israel"), [New Zealand](/wiki/Politics_of_New_Zealand "Politics of New Zealand"), [Denmark](/wiki/Politics_of_Denmark "Politics of Denmark")), [parallel voting](/wiki/Parallel_voting "Parallel voting") (e.g. [Japan](/wiki/Politics_of_Japan "Politics of Japan"), [Italy](/wiki/Politics_of_Italy "Politics of Italy")), or [preferential voting](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting") (e.g. [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Politics_of_Papua_New_Guinea "Politics of Papua New Guinea"), [Australia](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia")).
* A [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") of parliament with the ability to dismiss a government by "[withholding (or blocking) supply](/wiki/Loss_of_supply "Loss of supply")" (rejecting a budget), passing a [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence"), or defeating a [confidence motion](/wiki/Motion_of_Confidence "Motion of Confidence").
* A parliament that can be [dissolved](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament"), allowing [snap elections](/wiki/Snap_election "Snap election") to be called at any time.
* [Parliamentary privilege](/wiki/Parliamentary_privilege "Parliamentary privilege"), which allows the legislature to discuss any issue it deems relevant without fear of consequences stemming from defamatory statements or records thereof.
* Minutes of meetings, often known as [Hansard](/wiki/Hansard "Hansard"), including an ability for the legislature to strike discussion from these minutes.
* The ability of courts to address silence or ambiguity in the [statutory law](/wiki/Statutory_law "Statutory law") through the development of [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law"). Another parallel system of legal principles also exists known as [equity](/wiki/Equity_(legal_concept) "Equity (legal concept)"). Exceptions to this include India, Quebec in Canada, and Scotland in the UK, among other countries who mix common law with other legal systems.

Most of the procedures of the Westminster system originated with the [conventions](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)"), practices, and [precedents](/wiki/Precedent "Precedent") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), which form a part of what is known as the [Constitution of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom"). Unlike the [uncodified](/wiki/Uncodified_constitution "Uncodified constitution") British constitution, most countries that use the Westminster system have [codified](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") the system, at least in part, in a written [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution").

However, uncodified conventions, practices, and precedents continue to play a significant role in most countries, as many constitutions do not specify important elements of procedure. For example, some older constitutions using the Westminster system do not mention the existence of the cabinet or the prime minister, because these offices were taken for granted by the authors of these constitutions. Sometimes these conventions, [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_powers "Reserve powers"), and other influences collide in times of crisis and in such times the weaknesses of the unwritten aspects of the Westminster system, as well as the strengths of the Westminster system's flexibility, are put to the test. As an illustrative example, in the [Australian constitutional crisis of 1975](/wiki/1975_Australian_constitutional_crisis "1975 Australian constitutional crisis"), the Governor-General of Australia, [Sir John Kerr](/wiki/Sir_John_Kerr "Sir John Kerr"), dismissed Prime Minister [Gough Whitlam](/wiki/Gough_Whitlam "Gough Whitlam") and replaced him with opposition leader [Malcolm Fraser](/wiki/Malcolm_Fraser "Malcolm Fraser").

## Operation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Operation")]

The pattern of executive functions within a Westminster system is quite complex. In essence, the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_State "Head of State"), usually a [monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") or president, is a ceremonial figurehead who is the theoretical, nominal, or *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* source of executive power within the system. In practice, such a figure does not actively exercise executive powers, even though executive authority is nominally exercised in their name.

The [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), usually called the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") or [premier](/wiki/Premier "Premier"), will ideally have the support of a majority in the responsible house, and must, in any case, be able to ensure the existence of no [absolute majority](/wiki/Majority "Majority") against the government. If the parliament passes a [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence"), or refuses to pass an important [bill](/wiki/Bill_(law) "Bill (law)") such as the [budget](/wiki/Budget "Budget"), then the government must either resign so that a different government can be appointed or seek a [parliamentary dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") so that new general elections may be held in order to re-confirm or deny the government's mandate.

Executive authority within a Westminster system is de jure exercised by the cabinet as a whole, along with more junior [ministers](/wiki/Minister_(government) "Minister (government)"), however, in effect, the head of government dominates the executive as the head of government is ultimately the person from whom the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") will take [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional) "Advice (constitutional)") (by constitutional convention) on the exercise of [executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power"), including the appointment and dismissal of cabinet members. This results in the situation where individual cabinet members in effect serve at the pleasure of the prime minister. Thus the cabinet is strongly subordinate to the prime minister as they can be replaced at any time, or can be moved ("demoted") to a different portfolio in a [cabinet reshuffle](/wiki/Cabinet_reshuffle "Cabinet reshuffle") for "underperforming".

In the United Kingdom, the sovereign theoretically holds executive authority, even though the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") and the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom") effectively implement executive powers. In a [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic") like India, the [president](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") is the *de jure* executive, even though executive powers are essentially instituted by the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_India "Prime Minister of India") and the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Indian_Cabinet_Ministers "Indian Cabinet Ministers"). In [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), however, executive power is vested *de jure* and *de facto* in the cabinet and the [president](/wiki/President_of_Israel "President of Israel") is *de jure* and *de facto* a ceremonial figurehead.

As an example, the prime minister and cabinet (as the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* executive body in the system) generally must seek the permission of the head of state when carrying out executive functions. If, for instance the [British prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") wished to [dissolve Parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") in order for a [general election](/wiki/General_election "General election") to take place, the prime minister is constitutionally bound to request permission from the [sovereign](/wiki/British_monarchy "British monarchy") in order to attain such a wish. However, the sovereign, in modern times, has virtually always followed the advice of their prime minister without their own agency. This owes to the fact that the British sovereign is a [constitutional monarch](/wiki/Constitutional_monarch "Constitutional monarch"). The monarch abides by the advice of their ministers, except when executing [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power") in times of crisis. The sovereign's power to appoint and dismiss governments, appoint cabinet [ministers](/wiki/Minister_(government) "Minister (government)") to serve in the government, appoint [diplomats](/wiki/Diplomat "Diplomat"), declare [war](/wiki/War "War"), and to sign [treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") (among other powers de jure held by the sovereign) is known as the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"), which in modern times is exercised by the sovereign solely on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional) "Advice (constitutional)") of the Prime Minister.

This custom also occurs in other countries are regions around the world using the Westminster System, as a legacy of [British colonial rule](/wiki/British_colonial_rule "British colonial rule"). In [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") such as Canada, Australia and New Zealand, the day-to-day functions that would be exercised by the sovereign personally in the United Kingdom are instead exercised by the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-general "Governor-general"). In such nations, the prime minister is obligated to formally seek permission from the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-general "Governor-general") when implementing executive decisions, in a manner similar to the British system.

An analogous scenario also exists in [republics in the Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Republics_in_the_Commonwealth_of_Nations "Republics in the Commonwealth of Nations"), such as [India](/wiki/India "India") or [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), where there is a president who functions similarly to a governor-general.

An unusual case lies in [Israel](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Israel "Prime Minister of Israel") and [Japan](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan"), where the respective prime ministers have the full legal power to implement executive decisions, and [presidential (in Israel)](/wiki/President_of_Israel "President of Israel") or [imperial (in Japan)](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") approval is not required; the prime ministers of these nations are fully the *de jure* source of executive authority, and not the head of state.

The head of state will often hold meetings with the head of government and cabinet, as a means of keeping abreast of governmental policy and as a means of advising, consulting and warning ministers in their actions. Such a practice takes place in the United Kingdom and India. In the UK, the sovereign holds confidential weekly meetings with the prime minister to discuss governmental policy and to offer their opinions and advice on issues of the day. In India, the prime minister is constitutionally bound to hold regular sessions with the president, in a similar manner to the aforementioned British practice. In essence, the head of state, as the theoretical executive authority, "reigns but does not rule". This phrase means that the head of state's role in government is generally ceremonial and as a result does not directly institute executive powers. The [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_powers "Reserve powers") of the head of state are sufficient to ensure compliance with some of their wishes. However, the extent of such powers varies from one country to another and is often a matter of controversy.

Such an executive arrangement first emerged in the United Kingdom. Historically, the [British sovereign](/wiki/British_Sovereign "British Sovereign") held and directly exercised all executive authority. [George I of Great Britain](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") (reigned 1714 to 1727) was the first British monarch to delegate some executive powers to a prime minister and a cabinet of the ministers,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] largely because he was also the monarch of [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover") in Germany and did not speak English fluently. Over time, further arrangements continued to allow the execution of executive authority on the sovereign's behalf and more and more de facto power ended up lying in the [Prime Minister's](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") hands. Such a concept was reinforced in *[The English Constitution](/wiki/The_English_Constitution "The English Constitution")* (1876) by [Walter Bagehot](/wiki/Walter_Bagehot "Walter Bagehot"), who distinguished between the separate "dignified" and "efficient" functions of government. The sovereign should be a focal point for the [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") ("dignified"), while the PM and cabinet actually undertook executive decisions ("efficient").[[7]](#cite_note-Bagehot-1867-7)

## Electoral system, ministers and officials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Electoral system, ministers and officials")]

The [electoral system](/wiki/Electoral_system "Electoral system") is often set out in a [Representation of the People Act](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act "Representation of the People Act").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Common ministerial titles include [parliamentary secretary](/wiki/Parliamentary_secretary "Parliamentary secretary") and [under-secretary](/wiki/Undersecretary "Undersecretary"). Ministers are supported by [private secretaries](/wiki/Private_secretary "Private secretary") and government departments are run by [permanent secretaries](/wiki/Permanent_secretary "Permanent secretary"), [principal secretaries](/wiki/Principal_Secretary_(disambiguation) "Principal Secretary (disambiguation)") or [chief secretaries](/wiki/Chief_Secretary_(disambiguation) "Chief Secretary (disambiguation)").

## Role of the head of state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Role of the head of state")]

The [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_State "Head of State") or their representative (such as a [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-general "Governor-general")) formally appoints as the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") whoever commands the [confidence](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") of the [lower](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") or [sole house](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") of the legislature and invites him or her to form a government. In the UK, this is known as [kissing hands](/wiki/Kissing_hands "Kissing hands"). Although the dissolution of the legislature and the call for new elections is formally performed by the head of state, the head of state, by convention, acts according to the wishes of the head of government.

A president, monarch, or governor-general might possess clearly significant [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power"). Examples of the use of such powers include the [Australian constitutional crisis of 1975](/wiki/Australian_constitutional_crisis_of_1975 "Australian constitutional crisis of 1975") and the Canadian [King–Byng affair](/wiki/King%E2%80%93Byng_affair "King–Byng affair") in 1926. The [Lascelles Principles](/wiki/Lascelles_Principles "Lascelles Principles") were an attempt to create a convention to cover similar situations, but have not been tested in practice. Because of differences in their written constitutions, the formal powers of monarchs, governors-general, and presidents vary greatly from one country to another. However, as sovereigns and governors-general are not elected, and some presidents may not be directly elected by the people, they are often shielded from any public disapproval stemming from unilateral or controversial use of their powers.

In many [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms") a governor-general formally represents the monarch, who is usually absent from the realm. In such countries, the identity of the "head of state" may be unclear.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Cabinet government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cabinet government")]

Main article: [Cabinet collective responsibility](/wiki/Cabinet_collective_responsibility "Cabinet collective responsibility")

In the book *[The English Constitution](/wiki/The_English_Constitution "The English Constitution")*, [Walter Bagehot](/wiki/Walter_Bagehot "Walter Bagehot") emphasised the divide of the constitution into two components, the Dignified (that part which is symbolic) and the Efficient (the way things actually work and get done), and called the Efficient "[Cabinet Government](/wiki/Cabinet_Government "Cabinet Government")".[[7]](#cite_note-Bagehot-1867-7)

Members of the Cabinet are collectively seen as responsible for government policy, a policy termed [cabinet collective responsibility](/wiki/Cabinet_collective_responsibility "Cabinet collective responsibility"). All Cabinet decisions are made by consensus, a vote is rarely taken in a Cabinet meeting. All ministers, whether senior and in the Cabinet, or junior ministers, must support the policy of the government publicly regardless of any private reservations. When a [Cabinet reshuffle](/wiki/Cabinet_reshuffle "Cabinet reshuffle") is imminent, a lot of time is taken up in the conversations of politicians and in the news media, speculating on who will, or will not, be moved in and out of the Cabinet by the Prime Minister, because the appointment of ministers to the Cabinet, and threat of dismissal from the Cabinet, is the single most powerful constitutional power which a Prime Minister has in the political control of the Government in the Westminster system.

The [Official Opposition](/wiki/Official_Opposition "Official Opposition") and other major political parties not in the Government, will mirror the governmental organisation with their own [Shadow cabinet](/wiki/Shadow_cabinet "Shadow cabinet") made up of Shadow Ministers.

## Bicameral and unicameral parliaments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bicameral and unicameral parliaments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Canadian_Parliament_at_night.jpg/250px-Canadian_Parliament_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canadian_Parliament_at_night.jpg)

Canadian Parliament at night

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Glimpses_of_the_new_Parliament_Building%2C_in_New_Delhi_%282%29.jpg/250px-Glimpses_of_the_new_Parliament_Building%2C_in_New_Delhi_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glimpses_of_the_new_Parliament_Building,_in_New_Delhi_(2).jpg)

The [Sansad Bhavan](/wiki/Parliament_House_(India) "Parliament House (India)") (Parliament House) building in [New Delhi, India](/wiki/New_Delhi,_India "New Delhi, India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Knesset_Building_%28South_Side%29.JPG/250px-Knesset_Building_%28South_Side%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Knesset_Building_(South_Side).JPG)

[Knesset Building](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem")

In a Westminster system, some members of parliament are elected by popular vote, while others are appointed. Nearly all Westminster-based parliaments have a [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") with powers based on those of the [House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") (under various names), comprising local, elected representatives of the people (with [the only exception](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") being elected entirely by nationwide Proportional Representation). Most also have a smaller upper house, which is made up of members chosen by various methods:

* Termless appointees, either lifetime or retiring, from successive prime ministers (such as the [Senate of Canada](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"))
* Appointees of the premier and the opposition leader (such as the [Jamaican Senate](/wiki/Jamaican_Senate "Jamaican Senate"))
* Direct election (such as the [Australian Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate"))
* Election by electoral colleges or sub-national legislatures (such as the Indian [Rajya Sabha](/wiki/Rajya_Sabha "Rajya Sabha"))
* Hereditary nobility (such as the British [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") until the [House of Lords Act 1999](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Act_1999 "House of Lords Act 1999"))
* Any combination of the above (such as the Malaysian [Senate](/wiki/Dewan_Negara "Dewan Negara"))

* A prime minister can be elected without gaining a majority of the popular vote.

In the UK, the lower house is the *de facto* legislative body, while the upper house practices restraint in exercising its constitutional powers and serves as a consultative body. In other Westminster countries, however, the upper house can sometimes exercise considerable power, as is the case for the Australian Senate.

Some Westminster-derived parliaments are [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") for two reasons:

* The [New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament"), [Parliament of Queensland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Queensland "Parliament of Queensland"), and the parliaments of the Canadian provinces of [Manitoba](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Manitoba "Legislative Council of Manitoba"), [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick_Legislative_Council "New Brunswick Legislative Council"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Nova_Scotia "Legislative Council of Nova Scotia"), [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Prince_Edward_Island "Legislative Council of Prince Edward Island"), and [Quebec](/wiki/Qu%C3%A9bec "Québec") have abolished their upper houses.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* The parliaments of all other Canadian provinces, the [Parliament of Malta](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malta "Parliament of Malta"), the [Papua New Guinea Parliament](/wiki/National_Parliament_of_Papua_New_Guinea "National Parliament of Papua New Guinea"), the [Legislative Council of Hong Kong](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong") and the [Israeli Parliament](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") never had upper houses.

[Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), a former British [crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") and currently a [special administrative region](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China"), has a unicameral [Legislative Council](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong"). While the Legislative Councils in British Australasian and North American colonies were unelected upper houses and some of them had since abolished themselves, the Legislative Council of Hong Kong has remained the sole chamber and had in 1995 evolved into a fully elected house, yet only 20 of the 90 seats are returned by universal suffrage. Responsible government was never granted during British colonial rule, and the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Hong_Kong "Governor of Hong Kong") remained the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") until the transfer of sovereignty in 1997, when the role was replaced by the [Chief Executive](/wiki/Chief_Executive_(Hong_Kong) "Chief Executive (Hong Kong)"). Secretaries had remained to be chosen by the Chief Executive not from the Legislative Council, and their appointments need not be approved by the Legislative Council. Although essentially more presidential than parliamentary, the Legislative Council had inherited many elements of the Westminster system, including parliamentary powers, privileges and immunity, and the right to conduct inquiries, amongst others. The theme colour of the meeting chamber is red as in other upper houses. The Chief Executive may dissolve the Legislative Council under certain conditions, and is obliged to resign, e.g., when a re-elected Legislative Council passes again a bill that he or she had refused to sign.

## "Washminster system"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: \"Washminster system\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Australian_Senate_-_Parliament_of_Australia.jpg/250px-Australian_Senate_-_Parliament_of_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Australian_Senate_-_Parliament_of_Australia.jpg)

The [Australian Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate")

> The waters of the [Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") and of the [Potomac](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") both flow into [Lake Burley Griffin](/wiki/Lake_Burley_Griffin "Lake Burley Griffin").

— [James Killen](/wiki/James_Killen "James Killen"), [Farewell Parliament House - Sunday May 8, 1988](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=flzQwp8_y7g&t=637s)

[Australian constitutional law](/wiki/Australian_constitutional_law "Australian constitutional law") is, in many respects, a unique hybrid with influences from the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") as well as from the traditions and conventions of the Westminster system and some indigenous features. Australia is exceptional because the government faces a fully elected upper house, the [Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate"), which must be willing to pass all its legislation. Although government is formed in the lower house, the House of Representatives, the support of the Senate is necessary in order to govern.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The Australian Senate is unusual in that it maintains an ability to [withhold supply](/wiki/Loss_of_supply "Loss of supply") from the government of the day – a power similar to that held in the UK [until 1911 by the House of Lords](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911"), which has since then been impossible, in the Westminster system. A government that has lost supply is severely restricted in its abilities to act; unless a solution can be negotiated and supply can be restored, such an occurrence would normally trigger a federal election. Since the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"), technically speaking, can dismiss a federal government at any time, loss of supply is sometimes, controversially, considered a suitable trigger for a dismissal (such as with the [1975 Australian constitutional crisis](/wiki/1975_Australian_constitutional_crisis "1975 Australian constitutional crisis")). This is controversial because it conflicts with the Westminster tradition of government by a party with the confidence of the lower house (not an upper house like the Senate). Some political scientists have held that the [Australian system of government](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia") was consciously devised as a blend or hybrid of the Westminster and the [United States systems of government](/wiki/Federal_Government_of_the_United_States "Federal Government of the United States"), especially since the Australian Senate is a powerful upper house like the US Senate; this notion is expressed in the nickname "the Washminster mutation".[[18]](#cite_note-18) The ability of upper houses to block supply also features in the [parliaments of most Australian states](/wiki/Parliaments_of_the_Australian_states_and_territories "Parliaments of the Australian states and territories").

The Australian system has also been referred to as a [semi-parliamentary system](/wiki/Semi-parliamentary_system "Semi-parliamentary system").[[19]](#cite_note-Ganghof-19)

## Ceremonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ceremonies")]

The Westminster system has a very distinct appearance when functioning, with many British customs incorporated into day-to-day government function. A Westminster-style parliament is usually a long, rectangular room, with two rows of seats and desks on either side. Many chambers connect the opposing rows, either with a perpendicular row of seats and desks at the furthermost point from the Speaker's Chair at the opposite end of the chamber (e.g. UK House of Lords or Israel Knesset) or the rows of chairs and desks are rounded at the end, opposite to the Speaker's Chair (e.g. Australian chambers, Ireland, South Africa, India). The chairs in which both the government and opposition sit, are positioned so that the two rows are facing each other. This arrangement is said to have derived from an early Parliament which was held in a [church](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") [choir](/wiki/Choir_(architecture) "Choir (architecture)"). Traditionally, the opposition parties will sit in one row of seats, and the government party will sit in the other. In some countries, the mace will face the government's side whilst lying on the [table of the House](/wiki/Table_of_the_House "Table of the House"). In most [majority governments](/wiki/Majority_government "Majority government"), the number of government-party MPs is so large that it must use the "opposition" seats as well. In the lower house at Westminster (the UK's House of Commons) there are lines on the floor in front of the government and opposition benches that members may cross only when exiting the chamber.

At one end of the room sits a large chair, for the [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)"). The speaker usually wears black robes, and in some countries, a [wig](/wiki/Wig_(hair) "Wig (hair)"). Robed parliamentary [clerks](/wiki/Clerk_(legislature) "Clerk (legislature)") often sit at narrow tables between the two rows of seats, as well. These narrow tables in the centre of the chamber are usually where ministers or members of the house come to speak. A newly elected Speaker is symbolically dragged to the Chair upon being elected.

Other ceremonies sometimes associated with the Westminster system include an annual [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_Throne "Speech from the Throne") (or equivalent thereof) in which the head of state gives a special address (written by the government) to parliament about what kind of policies to expect in the coming year, and lengthy [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") ceremonies that often involve the presentation of a large [ceremonial mace](/wiki/Ceremonial_mace "Ceremonial mace"). Some legislatures retain Westminster's colour-coded chambers, with the upper houses associated with the colour red (after the House of Lords) and the lower with green (after the House of Commons). This is the case in India, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, and Barbados.

## Former countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Westminster_system&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Former countries")]

The Westminster system was adopted by a number of countries which subsequently evolved or reformed their system of government departing from the original model. In some cases, certain aspects of the Westminster system were retained or codified in their constitutions. For instance [South Africa](/wiki/Government_of_South_Africa "Government of South Africa") and [Botswana](/wiki/Politics_of_Botswana "Politics of Botswana"), unlike Commonwealth realms or parliamentary republics such as India, have a combined head of state and head of government but the President remains responsible to the lower house of parliament; it elects the President at the beginning of a new Parliament, or when there is a vacancy in the office, or when the sitting President is defeated on a vote of confidence. If the Parliament cannot elect a new President within a short period of time (a week to a month) the lower house is dissolved and new elections are called.
